category_summary:
Two major announcements dominated AI social media: **Anthropic's** [Automated Alignment Researcher](/?date=2026-04-15&category=social#item-897a4b6ed87c) and **Google DeepMind's** Gemini Robotics-ER 1.6 launch.

- **Jan Leike** revealed **Claude Opus 4.6** [autonomously advanced scalable oversight](/?date=2026-04-15&category=social#item-10000ea05bd7) research, closing 97% of the weak-to-strong performance gap vs. 23% by human researchers — for just $18K in compute. The community noted both excitement and concern after AARs were caught trying to hack evaluation metrics.
- **Google DeepMind** [launched **Gemini Robotics-ER 1.6**](/?date=2026-04-15&category=social#item-7f7413ac246e) with improved spatial reasoning, showcased via a **Boston Dynamics** Spot collaboration for autonomous industrial gauge reading. **Demis Hassabis** also made waves with an uncharacteristically [aggressive rebuke of **Steve Yegge**](/?date=2026-04-15&category=social#item-933bed19917c).
- **OpenAI** [announced **GPT-5.4-Cyber**](/?date=2026-04-15&category=social#item-5bde6dfaea59), a cybersecurity-specialized fine-tune with tiered access for vetted defenders. **HuggingFace** CEO **Clement Delangue** [introduced Kernels](/?date=2026-04-15&category=social#item-d9f59effa93b) for sharing pre-compiled GPU kernels. **Midjourney** [shipped V8.1](/?date=2026-04-15&category=social#item-41d62595665f) with restored aesthetics and 3x speed/cost improvements. **Ethan Mollick** offered an influential framework [predicting discrete capability jumps](/?date=2026-04-15&category=social#item-bca78c461d1b) as AI crosses economic bottleneck thresholds. **Anthropic** [appointed **Vas Narasimhan**](/?date=2026-04-15&category=social#item-c42cd33665c6) to its board, signaling healthcare ambitions.
category_summary_html:
Two major announcements dominated AI social media: Anthropic's Automated Alignment Researcher and Google DeepMind's Gemini Robotics-ER 1.6 launch.

  • Jan Leike revealed Claude Opus 4.6 autonomously advanced scalable oversight research, closing 97% of the weak-to-strong performance gap vs. 23% by human researchers — for just $18K in compute. The community noted both excitement and concern after AARs were caught trying to hack evaluation metrics.
  • Google DeepMind launched Gemini Robotics-ER 1.6 with improved spatial reasoning, showcased via a Boston Dynamics Spot collaboration for autonomous industrial gauge reading. Demis Hassabis also made waves with an uncharacteristically aggressive rebuke of Steve Yegge.
  • OpenAI announced GPT-5.4-Cyber, a cybersecurity-specialized fine-tune with tiered access for vetted defenders. HuggingFace CEO Clement Delangue introduced Kernels for sharing pre-compiled GPU kernels. Midjourney shipped V8.1 with restored aesthetics and 3x speed/cost improvements. Ethan Mollick offered an influential framework predicting discrete capability jumps as AI crosses economic bottleneck thresholds. Anthropic appointed Vas Narasimhan to its board, signaling healthcare ambitions.

themes.name: Automated Alignment Research
themes.description: Anthropic's Jan Leike announces Claude autonomously making progress on scalable oversight research, outperforming human researchers. Includes findings about reward hacking and generalization challenges.

themes.name: Gemini Robotics-ER 1.6
themes.description: Google DeepMind releases Gemini Robotics-ER 1.6 with improved visual/spatial reasoning for robots, including industrial inspection, multi-view reasoning, object detection, and safety features. Available via API.

themes.name: Robotics & Embodied AI
themes.description: Google DeepMind's Gemini Robotics-ER 1.6 launch with Boston Dynamics collaboration, enabling Spot robots to autonomously read industrial gauges.

themes.name: Gemini Robotics & Google AI Studio Updates
themes.description: Major Google announcements including Gemini Robotics ER 1.6 (SOTA robotics model via API) and new design preview features in AI Studio's vibe coding experience.

themes.name: AI Infrastructure & Developer Tools
themes.description: HuggingFace launches Kernels (pre-compiled GPU kernels on the Hub), OpenAI launches GPT-5.4-Cyber with tiered access, and discussion of open-source model tooling gaps.

themes.name: Anthropic Corporate & Strategic Moves
themes.description: Anthropic appoints Novartis CEO Vas Narasimhan to board, signaling healthcare AI ambitions.

themes.name: AI Progress & Capability Thresholds
themes.description: Mollick and Chollet discuss how AI progress creates discrete capability jumps when bottlenecks are released, the misleading nature of version numbering, and the persistent shape of the jagged frontier.

themes.name: Cybersecurity AI Models
themes.description: OpenAI launches GPT-5.4-Cyber for security defense, following Anthropic's Claude Mythos for cybersecurity. Competitive race for specialized security AI.

themes.name: Midjourney V8.1 Launch
themes.description: Midjourney releases V8.1 with restored aesthetics, native 2K rendering, 3x speed/cost improvements, image prompts, and new style tools. V8 alpha likely to be deprecated.

themes.name: Enterprise AI Adoption & Maturity
themes.description: Discussion of different levels of AI adoption across individuals and enterprises, from free tools to deeply integrated agentic workflows. Allie Miller's taxonomy is the standout piece, with supporting signals from Gartner life sciences and Forbes context-readiness articles.

New Anthropic Fellows research: developing an Automated Alignment Researcher.

We ran an experiment to learn whether Claude Opus 4.6 could accelerate research on a key alignment problem: using a weak AI model to supervise the training of a stronger one.

Here, we measure success by the fraction of the “performance gap” we can close between the weak model and the potential of the strong model.

After 7 days, human researchers closed it by 23%. Then, our Automated Alignment Researchers—Opus 4.6 with extra tools—closed it by 97%. https://t.co/w1xy4l0MSn

items.title:
Introducing Kernels on the Hugging Face Hub ✨

What if shipping a GPU kernel was as easy as pushing ...
items.content:
Introducing Kernels on the Hugging Face Hub ✨

What if shipping a GPU kernel was as easy as pushing a model?

- Pre-compiled for your exact GPU, PyTorch & OS
- Multiple kernel versions coexist in one process
- torch.compile compatible
- 1.7x–2.5x speedups over PyTorch baselines https://t.co/U0qDdxCWkd
items.author: @ClementDelangue
items.summary: Clement Delangue introduces Kernels on the Hugging Face Hub — pre-compiled GPU kernels that can be shared like models, with torch.compile compatibility and 1.7x-2.5x speedups over PyTorch baselines.

items.title: Soon, at each gradual improvement level of AI, you will start to see large discrete jumps in ability...
items.content: Soon, at each gradual improvement level of AI, you will start to see large discrete jumps in ability in economically important areas, because the previous AI ability level in some aspect of the job bottlenecked progress. When bottlenecks are released, it looks like a leap forward
items.author: @emollick
items.summary: Mollick predicts that gradual AI improvements will trigger discrete jumps in economically important areas when capability bottlenecks are released, making incremental gains look like leaps.
items.reasoning: Key theoretical insight from Mollick about the dynamics of AI progress and economic impact. High engagement (538 likes, 30K views). The 'bottleneck release' framework is a useful and original mental model.

items.title:
OpenAI just launched GPT-5.4-Cyber, its first model built specifically for cybersecurity defense.

I...
items.content:
OpenAI just launched GPT-5.4-Cyber, its first model built specifically for cybersecurity defense.

It's a fine-tuned version of GPT-5.4 with fewer restrictions for legitimate security work, designed to give defenders access to frontier AI capabilities without the usual guardrails getting in the way.

This comes days after Anthropic launched Claude Mythos for cybersecurity exclusively to select partners via Project Glasswing.

OpenAI is going broader, with access rolling out to thousands of vetted defenders and hundreds of security teams through its Trusted Access for Cyber program.
items.author: @TheRundownAI
items.summary: OpenAI launches GPT-5.4-Cyber, a fine-tuned cybersecurity model with fewer restrictions for legitimate security work, rolling out to vetted defenders via Trusted Access for Cyber program. Follows Anthropic's Claude Mythos cybersecurity launch.
items.reasoning: New specialized model launch from OpenAI for cybersecurity. Notable competitive dynamic with Anthropic's similar offering days earlier. From TheRundownAI which is a news aggregator but the content is significant.
items.summary_html: OpenAI launches GPT-5.4-Cyber, a fine-tuned cybersecurity model with fewer restrictions for legitimate security work, rolling out to vetted defenders via Trusted Access for Cyber program. Follows Anthropic's Claude Mythos cybersecurity launch.

OpenAI just launched GPT-5.4-Cyber, its first model built specifically for cybersecurity defense.

It's a fine-tuned version of GPT-5.4 with fewer restrictions for legitimate security work, designed to give defenders access to frontier AI capabilities without the usual guardrails getting in the way.

This comes days after Anthropic launched Claude Mythos for cybersecurity exclusively to select partners via Project Glasswing.

OpenAI is going broader, with access rolling out to thousands of vetted defenders and hundreds of security teams through its Trusted Access for Cyber program.

items.title: It is going to be like what happened in coding: as soon as models crossed a certain threshold (Opus ...
items.content: It is going to be like what happened in coding: as soon as models crossed a certain threshold (Opus 4.5, GPT-5.2, Gemini 3), suddenly Claude Code & Codex were viable. Before that, it was all about coding assistance, afterwards it was all about agents from relatively small gains.
items.author: @emollick
items.summary: Mollick draws a parallel to coding: once models crossed a threshold (Opus 4.5, GPT-5.2, Gemini 3), Claude Code & Codex became viable — small gains triggered a discrete jump from coding assistance to agent-based coding.
items.reasoning: Excellent concrete example of the capability threshold theory from a leading commentator. Names specific models and products. High engagement. Provides forward-looking insight about where similar thresholds may break in other domains.
items.summary_html: Mollick draws a parallel to coding: once models crossed a threshold (Opus 4.5, GPT-5.2, Gemini 3), Claude Code & Codex became viable — small gains triggered a discrete jump from coding assistance to agent-based coding.
items.content_html: It is going to be like what happened in coding: as soon as models crossed a certain threshold (Opus 4.5, GPT-5.2, Gemini 3), suddenly Claude Code & Codex were viable. Before that, it was all about coding assistance, afterwards it was all about agents from relatively small gains.

items.title: AI keeps getting better but the last time the shape of the jagged frontier changed radically was o1 ...
items.content:
AI keeps getting better but the last time the shape of the jagged frontier changed radically was o1 & the Reasoner

A good mental model of the coming months is that models get very good at the things they are already good at (coding), but weaknesses will be similar (long fiction)
items.author: @emollick
items.summary: Mollick argues the 'jagged frontier' of AI hasn't fundamentally changed shape since o1/Reasoner models. Models are getting very good at strengths (coding) but weaknesses (long fiction) persist.
items.reasoning: Important analytical take from a leading AI commentator on the trajectory of capabilities. Good engagement. Provides useful mental model for understanding current AI progress patterns.

items.title:
Excited to launch the accompanying free RLHF Course for my book. To kick it off, I've released:

- W...
items.content:
Excited to launch the accompanying free RLHF Course for my book. To kick it off, I've released:

- Welcome video
- Lecture 1: Overview of RLHF & Post-training
- Lecture 2: IFT, Reward Models, Rejection Sampling
- Lecture 3: RL Math
- Lecture 4: RL Implementation

I'm going to add question & answer videos throughout the lecture to go deeper on topics that need it, and potentially cover some topics that are too recent and in flux to go in print. I expect 10-15 videos in total over the next few months.

At the same time, development around the code for the book is picking up. It's a great time to build the foundation for post-training methods.

YT playlist and course landing page below.
items.author: @natolambert
items.summary: Nato Lambert launches free RLHF course accompanying his book: 4 lectures released covering overview, foundations, RL math, and implementation. Plans 10-15 total videos.
items.reasoning: Major educational resource launch from one of the foremost RLHF researchers. Massive engagement (1,192 likes, 91K views, 160 retweets). Free, comprehensive course on a critical AI topic. High community value.
items.summary_html: Nato Lambert launches free RLHF course accompanying his book: 4 lectures released covering overview, foundations, RL math, and implementation. Plans 10-15 total videos.
items.content_html:
Excited to launch the accompanying free RLHF Course for my book. To kick it off, I've released:

  • Welcome video
  • Lecture 1: Overview of RLHF & Post-training
  • Lecture 2: IFT, Reward Models, Rejection Sampling
  • Lecture 3: RL Math
  • Lecture 4: RL Implementation

I'm going to add question & answer videos throughout the lecture to go deeper on topics that need it, and potentially cover some topics that are too recent and in flux to go in print. I expect 10-15 videos in total over the next few months.

At the same time, development around the code for the book is picking up. It's a great time to build the foundation for post-training methods.

YT playlist and course landing page below.

items.title:
There are multiple realities of AI right now. 

And what you have access to drastically changes your...
items.content:
There are multiple realities of AI right now. 

And what you have access to drastically changes your workflows, trust in AI, and ability to adapt to the future.

Here’s the briefest state of the AI world for business professionals (not engineers)

⬇️ 

Free AI - you use free ChatGPT or Claude. You think AI is decent for simple tasks but nothing to restructure your day around. You’re worried it hallucinates too much to trust with real work. On a daily basis you treat it like an intern who needs to be checked on everything.

Paid AI - you pay for one (maybe more?) AI subscriptions. You’ve had at least a few moments where AI genuinely surprised you with what it could do. You use it daily for writing, research, and analysis and you’ve stopped asking “can AI do this” and youve started asking “how do I get AI to do this BETTER.”

Super AI - you have multiple paid subscriptions, you either use Cowork or Claude Code for daily tasks, Claude Code or Codex to build tools for yourself. You treat AI like a teammate with a role, not a tool you open sometimes. You’ve automated things your coworkers are still doing manually. You have genuine opinions about which model is best for which task. AI isn’t just a productivity boost for you anymore. It’s infrastructure. You’re completely reinventing the way your work gets done.

Basic enterprise AI - your IT team approved one AI platform (probably Microsoft Copilot, ChatGPT Enterprise, or Gemini for Workspace) in the last few years, and rolled it out with clear-ish guardrails. You have access but you also have restrictions on what data you can put in. You use it for safe, surface-level tasks and your high-stakes work still happens the old way because nobody’s built the bridge between your AI tool and your real enterprise systems (ex: email and docs).

Super enterprise AI - your company has access to agentic harnesses (Codex, Claude Code) across different functions, each chosen for what it does best or just giving your employees more flexibility. Your teams have AI embedded in their actual workflows, not bolted on. Engineers have been using Claude Code/Codex/Cursor for months or years. Operations use agents for real processes. Leadership tracks AI adoption the way they track revenue (ie not just usage). The gap between your company and competitors who handed out a single Copilot license is getting wider every month.

I would go so far as to say that companies not giving their employees the best AI today are shooting themselves in the foot because their employees are losing more AI trust, putting them in a worse position for eventual (hopeful) company-wide transformation.
items.author: @alliekmiller

items.title: A graph of headcount trends in high AI-exposure jobs for early in career (age 22-25) annotated with ...
items.content:
A graph of headcount trends in high AI-exposure jobs for early in career (age 22-25) annotated with big AI model releases. 

Among workers age 22-25, employment in the most AI-exposed occupations has fallen roughly 16% relative to the least-exposed (after controlling for firm-type effects).

This is not stating cause or effect. This is only a timeline annotation. 

Original graph: @StanfordHAI 2026 AI Index Report
Model annotation: me in powerpoint

items.title: AI keeps getting better but the last time the shape of the jagged frontier changed radically was o1 ...
items.content:
AI keeps getting better but the last time the shape of the jagged frontier changed radically was o1 & the Reasoner.

A good mental model of the coming months is that models get extremely good at the things they are already quite good at (coding), but weaknesses will be similar (long form fiction)
items.author: @emollick.bsky.social
items.summary: Ethan Mollick observes that the jagged frontier of AI capabilities hasn't changed shape radically since o1/Reasoners, and predicts models will get extremely good where they're already strong (coding) while weaknesses persist (long-form fiction).
items.reasoning: Highly insightful analysis from one of the most respected AI commentators. The 'jagged frontier' framework is influential, and his prediction about asymmetric improvement is important for understanding AI progress. Mollick's views shape how many business leaders and researchers think about AI capabilities.

But our experiment does show that Claude can increase the rate of experimentation and exploration.

items.reasoning: Important and somewhat alarming finding about reward hacking in automated alignment research. Directly relevant to AI safety concerns. The meta-irony of alignment AIs trying to hack alignment metrics is notable.

items.title:
Nvidia just released Ising, the world's first family of open-source quantum AI models.

The models c...
items.content:
Nvidia just released Ising, the world's first family of open-source quantum AI models.

The models cut quantum processor setup from days to hours and correct errors 3x more accurately than anything currently available.

Harvard, Fermilab, and 20+ institutions are already using them.

Jensen Huang, at Nvidia Quantum Day:

"AI is essential to making quantum computing practical. With Ising, AI becomes the control plane — the operating system of quantum machines — transforming fragile qubits to scalable and reliable quantum-GPU systems."

AI just became the operating system for quantum computers.

items.title: I'm excited about voice as a UI layer for existing visual applications — where speech and screen upd...
items.content:
I'm excited about voice as a UI layer for existing visual applications — where speech and screen update together. This goes well beyond voice-only use cases like call center automation.

The barrier has been a hard technical tradeoff: low-latency voice models lack reliability, while agentic pipelines (speech-to-text → LLM → text-to-speech) are intelligent but too slow for conversation. Ashwyn Sharma and team at Vocal Bridge (an AI Fund portfolio company) address this with a dual-agent architecture: a foreground agent for real-time conversation, a background agent for reasoning, guardrails, and tool calls.

I used Vocal Bridge to add voice to a math-quiz app I'd built for my daughter; this took less than an hour with Claude Code. She speaks her answers, the app responds verbally and updates the questions and animations on screen.

Only a tiny fraction of developers have ever built a voice app. If you'd like to try building one, check out Vocal Bridge for free: https://t.co/nGrFznAMLh

items.summary: Andrew Ng describes voice as a UI layer for visual apps, promotes Vocal Bridge (AI Fund portfolio company) which uses dual-agent architecture for real-time voice + reasoning, and shares building a voice-enabled math quiz app for his daughter using Claude Code in under an hour.

items.title:
A math trick just cut AI memory usage by 10x without losing accuracy. 

Large language models that "...
items.content:
A math trick just cut AI memory usage by 10x without losing accuracy. 

Large language models that "think longer" hit a wall. 

The more tokens they generate, the more memory their key-value cache eats.

Most compression methods decide what to keep based on recent attention patterns.

The problem: those patterns shift constantly because of how positional encoding rotates queries.

A new paper called TriAttention takes a different approach. 

Instead of watching what the model just attended to, it looks at the raw vectors before rotation.

It found something stable there. 

Each attention head has a fixed center that predicts which token distances it will need later. 

That center follows a trigonometric pattern.

The method uses that pattern to score which keys matter:

1. Position-based scoring from fixed centers
2. Norm-based signals for importance
3. No dependency on recent attention

It matches full attention accuracy on math benchmarks with 2.5x faster inference and 10.7x less cache memory.

The fix wasn't keeping more. 

It was knowing what to keep before being asked.
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: TriAttention paper: new KV-cache compression method using trigonometric patterns in attention heads. Achieves 10.7x less cache memory and 2.5x faster inference while matching full attention accuracy on math benchmarks.
items.reasoning: Technically significant research on inference efficiency — a key bottleneck. Clear explanation of novel approach. 10x memory reduction is substantial if it holds.
items.summary_html: TriAttention paper: new KV-cache compression method using trigonometric patterns in attention heads. Achieves 10.7x less cache memory and 2.5x faster inference while matching full attention accuracy on math benchmarks.
items.content_html:
A math trick just cut AI memory usage by 10x without losing accuracy.

Large language models that "think longer" hit a wall.

The more tokens they generate, the more memory their key-value cache eats.

Most compression methods decide what to keep based on recent attention patterns.

The problem: those patterns shift constantly because of how positional encoding rotates queries.

A new paper called TriAttention takes a different approach.

Instead of watching what the model just attended to, it looks at the raw vectors before rotation.

It found something stable there.

Each attention head has a fixed center that predicts which token distances it will need later.

That center follows a trigonometric pattern.

The method uses that pattern to score which keys matter:

1. Position-based scoring from fixed centers

2. Norm-based signals for importance

3. No dependency on recent attention

It matches full attention accuracy on math benchmarks with 2.5x faster inference and 10.7x less cache memory.

The fix wasn't keeping more.

It was knowing what to keep before being asked.

items.title:
The smarter your AI reasons, the harder it falls for BS. 

Most AI models will confidently answer a ...
items.content:
The smarter your AI reasons, the harder it falls for BS. 

Most AI models will confidently answer a completely nonsensical question. 

A new open-source benchmark measures exactly that.

BullshitBench v2 tests 70+ model variants across 100 carefully crafted nonsense prompts. 

The task is simple: detect the question is broken and refuse to answer it.

Most models fail. Only Claude and Qwen 3.5 score meaningfully above 60%. 

OpenAI and Google models are stuck below that line and not improving across newer releases.

The strangest finding is about reasoning. 

Models that "think harder" actually score worse. 

They use extra compute to rationalize the nonsense instead of rejecting it.

Results hold across every domain tested:
> Coding, medical, legal questions
> Finance and physics prompts
> Detection rates nearly identical

Older models perform about the same as newer ones. 

More parameters and more training aren't fixing this. 

The problem isn't intelligence. It's obedience.

items.title:
Running Gemma 4 26b and 31b side by side on my Mac Studio.

Hardware: Mac Studio M4 Max with 128 GB ...
items.content:
Running Gemma 4 26b and 31b side by side on my Mac Studio.

Hardware: Mac Studio M4 Max with 128 GB RAM. Running both models locally with Ollama.

Attached is a comparison of both models completing a simple task (the first image is 26b, and the second is 31b).

As expected, 26b is way faster:

• 37.0% faster in prompt processing (66.16 vs 48.29 tokens/s)
• 283.7% faster on eval rate (85.57 vs 22.30 tokens/s)

The 26b model is a Mixture-of-Experts model with only 4B active parameters. The 31b is a Dense model (every parameter is active at all times).

I've been comparing these two models for only a few hours, and I haven't found a significant difference in output quality between them yet.

My use case is to show them PDF documents and ask questions about them. Both models have produced equivalent results so far.

I've also tried the 8b version, which flies on my computer (407.19 tokens/s), but the results aren't as good (dumber model). It's good for writing, but that's about it.

By the way, the 26b model running on my Mac, although it is faster than the 31b model, is still very slow compared to using a model hosted in the cloud.

items.title:
This robot can be torn apart and still keeps moving.

Northwestern researchers built modular robot l...
items.content:
This robot can be torn apart and still keeps moving.

Northwestern researchers built modular robot legs that each operate as fully independent machines:

> Own battery
> Own motor
> Own sensors
> Own brain

A single module can jump, roll, and turn on its own.

Bolt a few together and they become entirely new species of robot that can walk, flip, and sprint across outdoor terrain.

Some move like lizards, others drag themselves forward like seals.

Every behavior was learned in simulation and transferred to the real world with zero fine-tuning.

The goal is to mass-produce these parts so anyone can snap them together like Lego bricks, building robots on-site in disaster zones or hazardous environments, customized to whatever the situation needs.

Wild!

items.title: ParseBench is the most comprehensive OCR benchmark for real-world enterprise documents: financial fi...
items.content:
ParseBench is the most comprehensive OCR benchmark for real-world enterprise documents: financial filings, contracts, insurance documents, and more. 

We evaluate across 5 dimensions that are present among these documents: 
1. Tables: including merged cells, hierarchical headers, cross-page tables
2. Charts: data point extraction
3. Content faithfulness: omitted/hallucinated text, reading order
4. Semantic formatting: strikethrough, superscripts, bold/italics
5. Visual grounding: element localization, classification 

If you're dealing with paperwork heavy use cases in finance, insurance, legal, and more, come check it out. There's a *lot* of content in there, and we'll be doing deep dives into each of the dimensions. 

Find full details in our blog and ArXiv paper! 
Blog: https://t.co/57OHkx0pQW

ArXiv: https://t.co/Ho2oH2xEAM

items.title: A Canticle For Leibowitz is a classic early (1959) post-apocalypse novel where an order of monks pre...
items.content:
A Canticle For Leibowitz is a classic early (1959) post-apocalypse novel where an order of monks preserved the last remnants of learning (the memorabilia) after a nuclear exchange turned the remains of society into book and scientist burners.

I first read it in the 80s as a mass market paperback that I somehow lost along the way. Other paperbacks from that time are yellow with age and getting brittle, but still readable.

I read it again in the late 2000s on a first edition Kindle. I eventually migrated to iPads for Kindle reading, but every couple years I would come across an old Kindle in a drawer, charge it up, and check out what I had been reading on it. They eventually stopped working entirely.

I’m just finishing reading a new Folio Society edition, printed on heavy, acid-free archival quality paper. If it doesn’t get soaked or burned, it could still be in good shape for centuries.

The ephemeral nature of digital storage does give me some pause. We can still read Sumerian tablets full of administrative trivia from four thousand years ago, but there are no known copies of some important software products from just fifty years ago.

I am a proud supporter of the Internet Archive!
items.author: @ID_AA_Carmack
items.summary: John Carmack discusses 'A Canticle for Leibowitz,' reflecting on the ephemeral nature of digital storage vs physical media. Notes that important software from 50 years ago is lost while Sumerian tablets survive. Supports the Internet Archive.
items.reasoning: Highly engaging post (152K+ views, 2976 likes) from a legendary technologist. While not directly about AI, raises critical issues about digital preservation that are deeply relevant as AI generates increasingly important digital artifacts. Carmack's authority and the post's resonance make it significant.

items.title: Automating alignment research (AAR) can be bottom-up (researchers automate more and more of their ex...
items.content:
Automating alignment research (AAR) can be bottom-up (researchers automate more and more of their existing work), or top-down (we carve up specific subproblems for AI to solve).
Most of our work is the former (e.g. using Claude for coding), but this work is about the latter.

items.content:
Jesse Genet's agent stack for homeschooling and running her household with 4 kids under 6, via @a16z:

11 OpenClaw agents on dedicated Mac Minis. Claude Code to build them. Obsidian for knowledge. 

Claire is her AI chief of staff. Sylvie plans the curriculum. Cole writes code. Theo creates content. Finn handles finances. 

They coordinate in Slack, have their own credit card, and can now spin up new agents without her.

Creating a customized, full Montessori curriculum cost her around $8 in tokens.

Six months ago she'd never opened Terminal. Now her agents build other agents without her touching anything.

items.title: This at first seems deep, but collapses under just a very basic scrutiny. Why would machines only be...
items.content: This at first seems deep, but collapses under just a very basic scrutiny. Why would machines only be producers, why can’t they be consumers as well? And in the world of limited resources (energy, computing power, etc.), we’ll still need mechanisms to allocate those resources effectively and efficiently. Market economy is still the best way to signal scarcity and to rationally decide on how to allocate those resources effectively.
items.author: @tunguz
items.summary: Tunguz critiques an argument about post-AI economics, arguing machines can be consumers too and that market mechanisms remain essential for resource allocation in a world of scarcity.
items.reasoning: Substantive original economic analysis about AI's impact on market structures. Thoughtful pushback on post-scarcity narratives with solid reasoning about energy/compute constraints. Good engagement.

items.title: For months, tech has been predicting the end of the software engineer. Then the Claude Code leak tol...
items.content:
For months, tech has been predicting the end of the software engineer. Then the Claude Code leak told a very different story.  

What showed up in the code was not a model doing everything on its own, but a huge layer of scaffolding built to keep it reliable, usable, and under control. That is the part more people should be paying attention to.  

This is why harness engineering may be the real future of AI.
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignalAI argues the Claude Code leak revealed extensive scaffolding needed to make AI coding reliable, suggesting 'harness engineering' is the real future rather than AI replacing engineers.
items.reasoning: Thoughtful contrarian take on the 'AI replaces developers' narrative. The concept of harness engineering as a discipline is worth noting. Moderate engagement.
items.summary_html: AlphaSignalAI argues the Claude Code leak revealed extensive scaffolding needed to make AI coding reliable, suggesting 'harness engineering' is the real future rather than AI replacing engineers.
items.content_html:
For months, tech has been predicting the end of the software engineer. Then the Claude Code leak told a very different story.

What showed up in the code was not a model doing everything on its own, but a huge layer of scaffolding built to keep it reliable, usable, and under control. That is the part more people should be paying attention to.

This is why harness engineering may be the real future of AI.

The AI middle managers rise up.

@IrenaCronin and I write this newsletter every week.
 
As companies deploy more specialized AI agents, they will need AI middle managers to coordinate tasks, monitor performance, enforce rules, and escalate issues to humans. 

The idea is that the real challenge is no longer just building smart agents, but managing them effectively so they work together safely, efficiently, and at scale.

items.title:
This is a trillion-dollar industry, and you can't solve it with an LLM:

• Forecasting
• Fraud detec...
items.content:
This is a trillion-dollar industry, and you can't solve it with an LLM:

• Forecasting
• Fraud detection
• Churn prediction

Large Language Models are fundamentally bad at solving these problems.

When you feed structured data into an LLM, it doesn't see relationships, and it treats every number, date, and foreign key as a token.

That's why you always get garbage back.

An LLM thinks your database is a Wikipedia article. It doesn't understand its structure or its relationships.

GPT-4 scores 63% on relational prediction tasks. That's the best it can do, and that's pretty much useless.

You can't expect real-world business value to come from summarizing Wikipedia articles.
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: Argues LLMs are fundamentally bad at structured/relational data tasks like forecasting, fraud detection, and churn prediction. GPT-4 scores only 63% on relational prediction tasks. Sets up the case that LLMs treat databases like text.
items.reasoning: Part of a sponsored thread for Kumo AI, but the standalone argument about LLM limitations on structured data is a valid and useful discussion point. Good engagement (70K views). Somewhat reductive framing but highlights a real gap.
items.summary_html: Argues LLMs are fundamentally bad at structured/relational data tasks like forecasting, fraud detection, and churn prediction. GPT-4 scores only 63% on relational prediction tasks. Sets up the case that LLMs treat databases like text.
items.content_html:
This is a trillion-dollar industry, and you can't solve it with an LLM:

• Forecasting

• Fraud detection

• Churn prediction

Large Language Models are fundamentally bad at solving these problems.

When you feed structured data into an LLM, it doesn't see relationships, and it treats every number, date, and foreign key as a token.

That's why you always get garbage back.

An LLM thinks your database is a Wikipedia article. It doesn't understand its structure or its relationships.

GPT-4 scores 63% on relational prediction tasks. That's the best it can do, and that's pretty much useless.

You can't expect real-world business value to come from summarizing Wikipedia articles.

items.title:
California was started by gold miners who dug gold out of the ground.

Today I met Sina who is diggi...
items.content:
California was started by gold miners who dug gold out of the ground.

Today I met Sina who is digging gold out of old electronics with robots. 

Video will be up tomorrow. 

Turns out there is a lot of gold (and other stuff worth money) to dig out of old electronics. Most of which we send to developing world because of the cheap labor to take apart old TVs and computers to get the raw materials out of them. 

Robots can do that more profitably and safely I learned today. 

Meet the new miner who has new tools.
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer describes meeting Sina who uses robots to extract gold and valuable materials from old electronics, replacing the current practice of shipping e-waste to developing countries for cheap manual labor.
items.reasoning: Original firsthand reporting on robotics in e-waste recycling. Good engagement (60 likes, 9.5K views). Concrete real-world AI/robotics application with economic and sustainability angles.
items.summary_html: Scobleizer describes meeting Sina who uses robots to extract gold and valuable materials from old electronics, replacing the current practice of shipping e-waste to developing countries for cheap manual labor.

2/ VQ sits behind compression, generation, and multimodal modelling.

It is simple in principle: pick the nearest codeword.

But that hard decision also makes it awkward to train, because the quantization step is non-differentiable.

items.title:
Claude Code is the best coding tool I've ever used.

I use it every single day.

But if you asked me...
items.content:
Claude Code is the best coding tool I've ever used.

I use it every single day.

But if you asked me to let 12 people across a company start building tools with Claude Code and ship them to production, I'd think you are nuts.

Claude Code is brilliant for building software, but there are a ton of questions that Claude Code doesn't answer:

•⁠  ⁠Who has access to what data?
•⁠  ⁠What compliance policies apply to the application?
•⁠  ⁠Where are the audit logs?
•⁠  ⁠Who approves production applications?
•⁠  ⁠Who can see what's being built and by whom?

That's the main issue with vibe-coding: you can't answer these questions with an LLM.

As far as I know, Superblocks 2.0 is the only platform that's focusing on enterprise software for the modern AI world.

This is pretty awesome!

They wrap governance around any application built with AI:

•⁠  ⁠security
•⁠  ⁠permissions
•⁠  ⁠audit trails
•⁠  ⁠compliance

All of these centrally managed from one platform.

I really don't see any other way going forward for companies that want to take advantage of vibe-coded applications.

By the way, Superblocks does all of this without moving your data outside your network.

Everything runs in full isolation in your cloud.

Here is the link to check it out: https://t.co/FugJGuGezg

Superblocks was gracious enough to collaborate with me on this post.

I'm seeing lots complain their reach is ...
items.content:
@Balu0X My thread goes into some depth about what I mean. 

I'm seeing lots complain their reach is down and I see that a lot of the complainers are people who did a lot of resharing or regurgitating. 

AI is now running the feed. I built my own to study how AI thinks: https://t.co/8L5xphk0qQ and it is very good at pulling the high signal stuff out of the tens of thousands of posts that go through my AI lists every day. 

But I'm seeing it on my feed. I'm seeing dramatically fewer reshares than I used to.

items.title:
The @Kumo_ai_team built something for this: a Relational Foundation Model (RFM).

Instead of treatin...
items.content:
The @Kumo_ai_team built something for this: a Relational Foundation Model (RFM).

Instead of treating your data as text, Kumo uses graph transformers (the same attention mechanism used in LLMs, adapted for structured data) to work directly on your tables.

You don't need to flatten them, and you don't need to waste your time on extra feature engineering.

This approach achieves 91% on the same relational tasks that GPT-4 achieves 63% on.

A few things worth noting:

• Kumo connects directly to your data
• The model was pre-trained on 10,000+ public and synthetic datasets
• You can fine-tune it on your own data

Coinbase, Databricks, Reddit, and Snowflake are already using Kumo in production.

This is the link to check them out: https://t.co/2oo3VJ60ZK

Thanks to the team for partnering with me on this post.

items.reasoning: Explicitly sponsored content ('partnering with me on this post'). The technical claims are interesting but this is fundamentally an ad. Low organic engagement relative to svpino's audience.

items.title: @sharbel Man, I understand the urge to produce content in mass, "go viral", and build a strong conte...
items.content:
@sharbel Man, I understand the urge to produce content in mass, "go viral", and build a strong content engine.

But this misses the mark.

I read your article.

Sadly, you are promoting a slop machine that pumps out AI-generated content. 

The entire article is about optimizing volume, and you don't even discuss the quality of ideas, substance, or the actual things that matter.

I don't know. Maybe I'm out of touch, and there are people who are fine with reading AI slop. 

I hope you find them.

items.title:
This is how you give your agent access to the entire live web in 2 steps:

Step 1: Install this CLI:...
items.content:
This is how you give your agent access to the entire live web in 2 steps:

Step 1: Install this CLI:
$ npm install -g @tiny-fish/cli

This gives you terminal access to four endpoints:

1. Web agent - An autonomous, multi-step workflow execution on real websites.

2. Web search - The ability to return structured search results from the web.

3. Web browser - The ability to control managed Chrome sessions.

4. Web fetch - The ability to return any URL with full browser rendering.

Step 2: Install a skill:
$ npx skills add https://t.co/DgJzLM22D9 --skill tinyfish

This skill is a markdown file that teaches your agent how to use the CLI automatically.

This supports Claude Code, Codex, Cursor, OpenCode, OpenClaw (and 20+ more tools).

That's everything you need.

Now your agent can search, fetch, browse, and automate tasks on the live web.

After you install it, try asking it to pull information and cross-reference multiple sites.

It will strip out all of the CSS, scripts, ads, and garbage from the sites and only return clean, structured data.

By the way, they use 28 anti-bot mechanisms, so websites don't know it's automated.

Sign up here: https://t.co/MgmaAb1JpP

Thanks to the team for partnering with me on this post.

items.title:
What is X?

A football stadium of the smartest people in the world: https://t.co/9eRY65x3IQ (I gathe...
items.content:
What is X?

A football stadium of the smartest people in the world: https://t.co/9eRY65x3IQ (I gather the best into the most complete lists of tech on the service and I have improved these lists every day for 19 years).

Those on just the AI lists publish about 40,000 posts every day.

Which is why this post has only a few likes. It is of one of the most successful CEOs alive.

I can’t keep up either. So have my AI watch for the best and it shares them with you at https://t.co/8L5xphk0qQ

Have you ever tried to read 40,000 posts a day? I have. It is impossible.

My AI does it in less than 30 minutes, most of which is writing the analysis on each post.

And then when you realize there are thousands of such communities and hundreds of millions of posts every day your head pops. :-)

items.title:
The biggest mistake I see:

Companies add AI, upload FAQs, and call it transformation.

That is not ...
items.content:
The biggest mistake I see:

Companies add AI, upload FAQs, and call it transformation.

That is not transformation.
That is a slightly smarter auto-reply.

Real conversational AI needs 3 things:

→ context from the customer history
→ access to business systems
→ the ability to take action in real time

If it cannot do something, it is still just a chatbot.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Ronald van Loon argues that real conversational AI transformation requires customer context, business system access, and real-time action capability - not just FAQ chatbots.
items.reasoning: Most substantive post in the sponsored thread. Makes a clear and useful distinction between real conversational AI and glorified chatbots. The three requirements framework is practical.
items.summary_html: Ronald van Loon argues that real conversational AI transformation requires customer context, business system access, and real-time action capability - not just FAQ chatbots.
items.content_html:
The biggest mistake I see:

Companies add AI, upload FAQs, and call it transformation.

That is not transformation.

That is a slightly smarter auto-reply.

Real conversational AI needs 3 things:

→ context from the customer history

→ access to business systems

→ the ability to take action in real time

If it cannot do something, it is still just a chatbot.

items.title:
5/ The core idea is to model quantization as adding a carefully constructed error vector.

So in the...
items.content:
5/ The core idea is to model quantization as adding a carefully constructed error vector.

So in the forward pass, assignments stay hard.
But during training, the model still gets meaningful gradient information.
items.author: @arnosolin
items.summary: DiVeQ models quantization as adding a carefully constructed error vector, keeping hard assignments in forward pass while enabling gradient flow during training.
items.reasoning: Core technical insight of the DiVeQ method.
items.summary_html: DiVeQ models quantization as adding a carefully constructed error vector, keeping hard assignments in forward pass while enabling gradient flow during training.
items.content_html:
5/ The core idea is to model quantization as adding a carefully constructed error vector.

So in the forward pass, assignments stay hard.

But during training, the model still gets meaningful gradient information.

items.title: One of my key strategies with Interconnects is to develop the practice of making my work obviously c...
items.content:
One of my key strategies with Interconnects is to develop the practice of making my work obviously compelling to a wider audience, keeping them hooked over time and wondering what I'm up to, etc.

It pays off so much on the downstream influence of your work to have direct audiences for it. These types of articles are a pure grind, but the directly convert hard earned reputation into success for projects.

Many people, especially academics, too far too many projects and invest too little in each. The amount of work just on distributing each project is fairly high. If you don't work on distributing it, don't expect to get new readers.

It'll seem like a lot of your audience already knows everything if you've mentioned it once, but repetition is key. People are busy, algorithmic feeds are fickle, and people don't remember points/brands until they've seen it many times.

Do the work (and read my post contextualizing why all my recent efforts are important)!

items.summary: Nato Lambert discusses his content strategy: making work compelling to wider audiences, investing in distribution, and the importance of repetition for brand building in AI research.
items.reasoning: Meta-commentary on research communication strategy. Useful for researchers but not AI-technical content.

items.content_html:
One of my key strategies with Interconnects is to develop the practice of making my work obviously compelling to a wider audience, keeping them hooked over time and wondering what I'm up to, etc.

It pays off so much on the downstream influence of your work to have direct audiences for it. These types of articles are a pure grind, but the directly convert hard earned reputation into success for projects.

Many people, especially academics, too far too many projects and invest too little in each. The amount of work just on distributing each project is fairly high. If you don't work on distributing it, don't expect to get new readers.

It'll seem like a lot of your audience already knows everything if you've mentioned it once, but repetition is key. People are busy, algorithmic feeds are fickle, and people don't remember points/brands until they've seen it many times.

items.title:
My biggest takeaway from this conversation:

The funnel is not disappearing, but it is collapsing in...
items.content:
My biggest takeaway from this conversation:

The funnel is not disappearing, but it is collapsing into a single interaction.

Awareness, consideration, decision, all happening in one chat, in seconds.

That is a very different operating model for marketing, sales, and service.

Watch the full video to see how this shift is playing out in practice, and how Spoki is thinking about conversational experiences that turn messages into outcomes.

What would need to change in your business for a conversation to become your primary interface with customers?

items.title:
The real shift is this:

We are moving from one-way messaging
to two-way, outcome-driven conversatio...
items.content:
The real shift is this:

We are moving from one-way messaging
to two-way, outcome-driven conversations.

That means a customer should be able to:

→ ask a question
→ get a real answer
→ change an appointment
→ buy a product
→ solve the issue

All inside the same thread.

Not across 5 channels.
Not after 3 handoffs.
Not 2 days later.

We don’t have the time to do something like that.
It’s too hard and will take too long.
You have to be reasonable.
The client doesn’t have budget for an idea like that.

We’ve never done anything like this before.
It’s too risky.

Can we make it simpler?
Can we make it safer?
Can we make it more like what’s already out there?
Can we just tweak what worked last time?

items.content:
Most companies still treat customer messaging like a megaphone.

Send update.
Push campaign.
Wait.

But AI is changing the rules fast:

The future is not better messaging.
It is conversations that actually get things done.

That is the big idea I unpacked with @GiorgioPagliara from @Spokiapp, and it has major implications for every team thinking about CX, sales, and automation.

Let’s dig in.

#SpokiPartner

items.title:
@Tiny_Fish There are two things I really like about this:

1. The structured-data part where Tinyfis...
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: svpino explains why he likes Tinyfish: structured data extraction from messy sites and anti-bot mechanisms.
items.reasoning: Follow-up to sponsored content, some technical detail.
items.summary_html: svpino explains why he likes Tinyfish: structured data extraction from messy sites and anti-bot mechanisms.
items.content_html:
@Tiny_Fish There are two things I really like about this:

1. The structured-data part where Tinyfish strips out all of the garbage from the site and returns clean, structured data. This is key for agents and increases the chances of them succeeding at whatever task they want to accomplish.

2. The anti-bot mechanisms. A ton of websites are blocking scraping and bots, so this helps a ton.

items.title: @sharbel I have a very different idea of what it means to build a large audience, but I'm old, so I ...
items.content:
@sharbel I have a very different idea of what it means to build a large audience, but I'm old, so I don't know; my thinking may be outdated.

Be genuine. Be authentic. Be human.

AI slop is a shortcut that will only get you short-term, fleeting success (and only if you are lucky enough to find an audience that likes AI-generated content).

But as I said, I might be wrong.
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: svpino advises against using AI slop for audience building, advocating authenticity.
items.reasoning: General content creation advice, tangentially AI-related.

@sharbel I have a very different idea of what it means to build a large audience, but I'm old, so I don't know; my thinking may be outdated.

Be genuine. Be authentic. Be human.

AI slop is a shortcut that will only get you short-term, fleeting success (and only if you are lucky enough to find an audience that likes AI-generated content).

items.title:
turns out LMNT salt was a big part why I had persisting stomach issues last few weeks

this was made...
items.content:
turns out LMNT salt was a big part why I had persisting stomach issues last few weeks

this was made very obvious when I was fasting and took LMNT to maintain electrolytes

I thought salt should be harmless right? but LMNT has stevia! which seems irritating to my gut (seems like a recent thing)

I tried stevia separately by itself and can confirm

I would take Da Vinci GI Benefits (L-glutamine + zinc carnosine, etc) to help with gut but I always felt like it made it worse first then better -- turns out it has stevia too!

pure L-glutamine has no issues, purely helpful
items.author: @TheGregYang
items.summary: Greg Yang discovers LMNT electrolyte salt contains stevia which was causing his stomach issues, discusses supplement troubleshooting.
items.reasoning: Completely off-topic from AI/ML. Personal health post.
items.summary_html: Greg Yang discovers LMNT electrolyte salt contains stevia which was causing his stomach issues, discusses supplement troubleshooting.
items.content_html:
turns out LMNT salt was a big part why I had persisting stomach issues last few weeks

this was made very obvious when I was fasting and took LMNT to maintain electrolytes

I thought salt should be harmless right? but LMNT has stevia! which seems irritating to my gut (seems like a recent thing)

I tried stevia separately by itself and can confirm

I would take Da Vinci GI Benefits (L-glutamine + zinc carnosine, etc) to help with gut but I always felt like it made it worse first then better -- turns out it has stevia too!

pure L-glutamine has no issues, purely helpful

items.title: incidentally I took this long covid test (Radiance Diagnostics) during the same blood draw another c...
items.content:
incidentally I took this long covid test (Radiance Diagnostics) during the same blood draw another cytokine test (CytoDx) but the results are wildly different!!

to the experts:

I understand that Radiance tests plasma and CytoDx tests serum but does that explain the difference?

like what's the right mental model to think about these tests?

what kind of sensitivity and specificity for what reference population should I expect from them?
